query: lymphatic drainage of the tongue anatomy

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Arterial Supply and Lymphatic Drainage of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The arterial supply to the tongue derives from branches of the external carotid artery, primarily through the lingual artery and the tonsillar branch of the facial artery. Venous drainage occurs through tributaries to the lingual vein. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The lymphatic vessels from the body of the tongue are divided into marginal and central. The marginal vessels drain the tongue's apex, marginal surface, and sublingual region into the submental and submandibular nodes. [10] Less often, they drain into the jugulodigastric or jugulo-omohyoid nodes. The central vessels are located at the tip of the tongue, travel through the mylohyoid muscle, and drain into the jugulo-omohyoid or jugulodigastric nodes. [10] The dorsal vessels collect lymphatic drainage from the area posterior to the sulcus and drain into the jugulo-omohyoid and jugulodigastric nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphatic drainage of the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is to the submental and submandibular lymph nodes, emptying into the deep cervical chain of lymph nodes. The posterior third of the tongue drains directly into the deep cervical lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Arterial Supply and Lymphatic Drainage of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: As a result, the burden of nodal disease is a predictor of increased mortality. Elective neck dissection has been shown to provide a survival advantage and higher disease-free survival rates compared to therapeutic neck dissection. [15] [16] Occasionally, lower lymph nodes in levels 3 and 4 can harbor metastases even when levels 1 or 2 do not show disease, indicating a need for more aggressive management. [17] Therefore, neck dissection is strongly recommended as it offers a more accurate evaluation of lymph node status.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Blood supply to the tongue comes predominantly from the lingual artery , a branch of the external carotid artery. [8] Venous drainage is done by the lingual vein .

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Arterial Supply and Lymphatic Drainage of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphatic drainage of the oral tongue primarily leads to levels 1 through 3 of the neck, which includes the submental (level 1a) and submandibular (level 1b) lymph node basins, as well as the upper jugular chain lymphatics (levels 2 and 3). Conversely, lymphatic drainage from the tongue base primarily flows to levels 2 through 4. [9] [10] Understanding the lymphatic system is crucial for the locoregional management of tongue cancers. Neck dissection should be considered in the treatment of squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue due to the risk of cervical lymph node metastases, even in clinically negative necks, which can harbor hidden metastases at a rate of 20%. [11] Additionally, tumor thickness is correlated with cervical lymph node metastases. [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The dorsal lingual artery arises beneath the hyoglossus muscle and passes to the posterior part of the dorsum of the tongue. It supplies the mucous membrane of this region as well as the glossopalatine arch, lingual tonsils, soft palate, and epiglottis. [9] They anastomose with their corresponding vessels on the opposite side.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lymph Nodes -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Drainage Level VIb drains the efferent lymphatics from the anterior floor of the mouth, tip of the oral tongue, lower lip, thyroid gland, glottic and supraglottic larynx, hypopharynx, and cervical esophagus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The suprahyoid artery supplies the omohyoid, sternothyroid, and thyrohyoid muscles. They anastomose with the corresponding vessels from the opposite side.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The deep lingual artery , which is the termination of the lingual artery, passes between the genioglossus muscle and inferior longitudinal muscle, supplying the apex of the tongue. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Artery -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, the length of the lingual artery is 75% of the length of the tongue, from the dorsal portion up to the tip. The path of the artery has a lateral and inferior direction for about three-quarters of its length.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The styloglossus muscle receives its blood supply from the sublingual branch of the lingual artery. The lingual artery is a direct branch of the external carotid artery; it takes off from the external carotid at the level of the greater horn of the hyoid bone [1] . After this, the lingual artery travels between the hyoglossus (superficial) and middle pharyngeal constrictor (deep) to supply blood to the tongue. Dorsal lingual arteries supply the base of the tongue, whereas deep lingual arteries supply the body of the tongue. [7] Venous drainage of the tongue is via deep lingual veins and a single dorsal lingual vein; these vessels drain into the sublingual vein, which in turn empties directly into the internal jugular vein.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The lingual artery arises from the external carotid artery at the tip of the hyoid bone's greater horn, between the facial and superior thyroid arteries. [9] [8] After branching from the external carotid artery, the lingual artery passes deep to the hyoglossus muscle and superficial to the middle pharyngeal constrictor muscle. It then gives rise to the following four arteries:

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palatoglossus Muscle (Glossopalatinus, Palatoglossal) -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The arterial supply is from the lingual artery, which is the branch of the external carotid artery. Some collateral circulation also comes from the branch of the facial artery (tonsillar artery). The lymphatic drainage is into the deep cervical group of lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Swallowing -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The submental group of nodes primarily drain the floor of the oral cavity and the anterior part of the tongue. The submandibular group drains both the hard and soft palates, the mucosa of both cheeks, and contributes to drainage of the mouth and anterior tongue. The upper jugular cluster of nodes drains the parotid gland and receives secondary drainage from the submental and submandibular node clusters, as well as the pharynx and larynx. The middle jugular cluster of nodes directly drains the base of the tongue, hypopharynx, as well as receiving drainage from the upper jugular cluster. The lower jugular group of nodes primarily drains efferent lymphatics from the upper and middle jugular nodes as well as drainage from the hypopharynx. The medial supraclavicular cluster of nodes receives drainage from the lower jugular nodes, as well as the hypopharynx and esophagus. The posterior triangle group of nodes drains the nasopharynx and oropharynx. The anterior compartment of nodes drains the anterior floor of the mouth, the anterior-most part of the tongue, hypopharynx, and cervical esophagus. The retropharyngeal nodes drain the nasopharynx and the soft palate, while the retrostyloid nodes drain just efferent lymph via the nasopharynx. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Anterior Cervical Region -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Lymphatic Drainage of the Anterior Cervical Region
retrieved_docs.content: Facial lymphatic drainage in the area includes the submandibular nodes, which are deep to the submandibular gland within the submandibular triangle and drain the oral cavity and lower face soft tissue structures. Another important lymphatic drainage basin of the anterior cervical neck is the submental lymph nodes, which lie in the submental triangle and drain the floor of the mouth, tongue apex, and lower lip. [9]

Thoracoscopic wedge resection of the lung targets the thoracic cavity as a lung‑sparing alternative to lobectomy/pneumonectomy and is linked anatomically to axillary lymph nodes (نقائل إبطية), a regional lymphatic drainage site relevant for staging thoracic and upper‑body pathologies. Injection of the larynx is identified as throat surgery and is associated with placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي), a non‑therapeutic respiratory system procedure that underlies respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation. Malignant neoplasm of the bronchus is likewise associated with the thoracic cavity, emphasizing that bronchial cancers arise within this compartment. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is recognized as a manifestation of head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق). This relationship indicates that the spread of malignant cells to the lymph nodes within the head and neck region is a specific form of head and neck cancer, highlighting its relevance for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

Direct procedures on the trachea include tracheoscopy via tracheostomy (temporary or permanent) and tracheal intubation (تنبيب الرغامى); these are classified under “other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum.” Indirect or therapeutic procedures on the trachea and larynx fall under “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system” and include throat surgery such as injection of the larynx and injection of a locally‑acting therapeutic substance into the trachea. retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694

This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2340
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node and metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck are both classified as secondary malignancies, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue in the head and neck region. In addition, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is directly linked to the morphological pattern described by the Arabic term “السرطان النقيلي,” which denotes metastatic cancer. Recognizing these entities as secondary malignancies and understanding their characteristic metastatic morphology are important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management of patients with head‑and‑neck cancer spread.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy; partial or complete, indicating that removal of the thyroid gland—whether in part or in entirety—is performed within this anatomical region. This procedure is also known by its Arabic name استئصال الدرقية, which serves as the parent term for the English description of thyroidectomy; partial or complete. Understanding that the thyroid gland resides in the head and neck area and recognizing both the English and Arabic terminology helps clinicians accurately plan and communicate surgical interventions for thyroid disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.
